title: NIS2 Supply Chain Third-Party Risk Continuous Monitoring
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel/competent-authority review.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.16` daily exports, ES asset snapshot IDs, and frozen bucket copies referenced in the third-party risk workpaper.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(d) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.16: NIS2 Supply Chain Third-Party Risk Continuous Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.16' running on index proxy and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Load accelerated fixtures where `All_Traffic.dest_category=supplier`, `bytes_out` exceeds one gigabyte per day, and `supplier_risk_lookup` shows `last_assessment_date` older than 400 days; the alert fires listing dest and overdue flag 1.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Feed the same traffic volumes but provide an updated assessment date within 90 days and a documented marketing campaign exception row; the search returns no rows after applying the lookup filter and exception table.

dataSources: Accelerated CIM `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` fed from `index=proxy` `sourcetype=bluecoat:proxystorage` or `sourcetype=zscaler:lss` with fields dest, bytes_out, src, app; `index=cloud` `sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail` (Splunk Add-on for AWS [Splunkbase 1876](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1876)) for partner-shared buckets; Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)) provides `dest_category=supplier` tagging via Assets & Identities.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)), Splunk Add-on for AWS ([Splunkbase 1876](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1876)); optional Splunk Add-on for Cisco Umbrella for DNS enrichment.
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out dc(All_Traffic.src) as internal_sources
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where All_Traffic.dest_category="supplier"
  by All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.app span=1d
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| lookup supplier_risk_lookup dest OUTPUT supplier_name, risk_tier, last_assessment_date
| eval days_since_assessment=round((now()-strptime(last_assessment_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400, 0)
| eval assessment_overdue=if(days_since_assessment > 365, 1, 0)
| where bytes_out > 1073741824 OR assessment_overdue=1 OR internal_sources > 50
| sort - bytes_out
| table dest, supplier_name, risk_tier, bytes_out, internal_sources, days_since_assessment, assessment_overdue
description: Uses accelerated network-traffic summaries tagged for supplier destinations to highlight oversized daily uploads, overdue third-party risk reviews, or an unusually wide set of internal clients talking to one supplier API.
value: Risk committees see spikes in data movement toward a vendor before a news-driven audit question lands, and they get a calendar view of which supplier reassessments slipped past 12 months without paperwork.
implementation: (1) In ES (263), assign `category=supplier` to third-party CIDRs/domains under **Configure → Data Enrichment → Asset Management**. (2) Build `supplier_risk_lookup` CSV with dest (IP or FQDN), supplier_name, risk_tier, last_assessment_date. (3) Enable **Network_Traffic** datamodel acceleration for **30 days** minimum. (4) Validate proxy field mapping to `All_Traffic.bytes_out`. (5) Add AWS CloudTrail (1876) panels if suppliers use cross-account roles.
visualization: Table of supplier rows with bytes_out and assessment flags, bar chart of top destinations, single value count of overdue assessments.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
• **Large overnight backup to a supplier-managed SaaS region** — Distinguish: backup software User-Agent known from allow list. Suppress: lookup `backup_supplier_dest.csv` with dest,app tuple.
• **Assessment date CSV stale while GRC tool holds truth** — Distinguish: `last_assessment_date` older than 400 days but Workday shows recent review. Suppress: sync automation from ServiceNow GRC; interim time-bound exception register.
• **CDN front door shared by many suppliers** — Distinguish: single `dest` maps to generic edge IP. Suppress: refine Assets to use **Fully Qualified Domain Name** from TLS SNI via Stream Add-on.
• **AWS cross-account telemetry mirrored into supplier category** — Distinguish: role assumption for telemetry, not data exfiltration. Suppress: `where All_Traffic.app!="sensor-forwarder"` after validation.
• **Seasonal e-invoicing traffic spike** — Distinguish: finance window predictable from calendar. Suppress: `where NOT (date_month=3 AND app="sap")` macro during close.
equipmentModels: Bluecoat/Symantec ProxySG (sourcetype bluecoat:proxystorage, bytes_out for upload monitoring)
Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876, CloudTrail cross-account role assumption events)
Splunk CIM Add-on (Splunkbase 1621, Network_Traffic data model normalisation)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Assets & Identities for dest_category tagging)
Zscaler Internet Access (sourcetype zscaler:lss, ZIA log streaming for cloud proxy telemetry)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC implements NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) — supply chain security, including security-related aspects concerning relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers. Art.21(2)(d) is one of the most scrutinised NIS2 provisions because supply chain attacks (SolarWinds, Kaseya, MOVEit) have demonstrated that an entity's security is only as strong as its weakest supplier. This UC provides continuous telemetry-based monitoring of data flows to supplier destinations, supplementing the traditional questionnaire-based assessment approach with real-time evidence.
• Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) with a licensed Assets & Identities framework. ES's asset model is the foundation: supplier IP ranges, domains, and API endpoints must be tagged with `category=supplier` in the asset database. Without this tagging, the `tstats` query's `where All_Traffic.dest_category="supplier"` filter returns zero results. Navigate to ES > Configure > Data Enrichment > Asset & Identity Management > Asset Lookups. Add a new asset lookup source (CSV or KV Store) with at minimum: `ip` (or `dns`), `category` (set to `supplier`), `priority` (critical/high/medium/low based on the supplier's risk tier), `owner` (the internal relationship manager). Reload the asset merge.
• Understanding the `dest_category=supplier` mechanism: ES's asset merge process runs periodically (default every 5 minutes). It reads all configured asset lookup sources, merges them by priority, and creates the `assets_by_str` lookup. When the CIM Network_Traffic data model processes an event with a `dest` IP or FQDN that matches an asset entry with `category=supplier`, it automatically populates `dest_category=supplier` on that event. This means the `tstats` query filters the entire network traffic dataset down to only supplier-bound traffic without any manual filtering — but only if the asset merge is healthy and the supplier inventory is complete.
• CIM Network_Traffic data model accelerated for at least 30 days. The data model must be fed by proxy logs (Bluecoat, Zscaler, Palo Alto URL filtering), firewall logs (Cisco ASA, Palo Alto, Fortinet), or Splunk Stream wire data. The critical CIM fields for this UC are: `dest` (destination IP or FQDN), `src` (source internal IP), `bytes_out` (bytes uploaded to the destination), `app` (application layer protocol or application name), `dest_category` (populated by ES asset merge). Verify acceleration: Settings > Data Models > Network_Traffic > Edit > Acceleration > Enabled, Summary Range ≥ 30 days.
• Proxy log data sources: the primary feed for this UC. Bluecoat/Symantec ProxySG logs (`sourcetype=bluecoat:proxystorage`) or Zscaler Internet Access logs (`sourcetype=zscaler:lss`) provide `bytes_out` for outbound data transfers. Both TAs map to the CIM Network_Traffic model. If using Zscaler, configure ZIA Log Streaming Service (LSS) to forward to a Splunk Heavy Forwarder via syslog or to HEC. If using Bluecoat, configure the proxy to write access logs in W3C Extended Log Format with the `sc-bytes` and `cs-bytes` fields.
• CSV lookup `supplier_risk_lookup.csv` with columns: `dest` (IP, CIDR, or FQDN matching the network traffic destination), `supplier_name` (human-readable name), `risk_tier` (critical/high/medium/low — based on the supplier's access to your data, systems, and the criticality of the service they provide), `last_assessment_date` (ISO 8601 date of the most recent third-party risk assessment), `contract_expiry` (optional — for contract lifecycle monitoring), `data_classification` (the highest classification of data shared with this supplier — public/internal/confidential/restricted). Store in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-supply-chain/lookups/` and version-control in Git.
• The `supplier_risk_lookup` must be maintained by procurement in collaboration with the GRC team. It should be refreshed after every supplier assessment (typically annually for low-risk, semi-annually for high-risk, quarterly for critical suppliers). The lookup is the "truth table" for assessment cadence — the SPL compares the current date against `last_assessment_date` to determine if the assessment is overdue.
• Index retention: the underlying proxy/firewall indexes (typically `index=proxy` or `index=firewall`) should have ≥ 90 days retention for trend analysis, though the accelerated data model summary provides the primary query path. `index=audit_evidence` ≥ 7 years for compliance evidence.
• RBAC: role `nis2_supply_chain_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = proxy, firewall, cloud, audit_evidence` and access to the `supplier_risk_lookup`. Supplier data flows may reveal confidential business relationships — restrict access appropriately.
• Optional: Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876) for CloudTrail monitoring of cross-account role assumptions. If suppliers access your AWS environment via IAM roles, CloudTrail events (`AssumeRole`, `AssumeRoleWithSAML`) provide evidence of supplier access patterns.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**ES Assets & Identities — supplier tagging:**
Create a supplier asset CSV with your third-party inventory. Minimum columns:
```
ip,dns,category,priority,owner,bunit
10.0.50.0/24,,supplier,critical,john.smith@example.com,procurement
,api.vendor-a.com,supplier,high,jane.doe@example.com,procurement
203.0.113.50,,supplier,medium,procurement-team@example.com,procurement
```

In ES: Configure > Data Enrichment > Asset & Identity Management > New Asset Lookup. Point to the CSV. Set merge priority appropriately (supplier assets should not override internal asset classifications).

Verify the asset merge:
```
| inputlookup assets_by_str
| search category=supplier
| stats count dc(ip) as ips dc(dns) as domains
```
Expected: `ips + domains` should match the number of supplier entries. If count is 0, the asset merge has not processed the new lookup — trigger a manual merge via `| sendalert update_es_assets` or wait for the next scheduled merge cycle.

Verify `dest_category` population in the data model:
```
| tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic where All_Traffic.dest_category="supplier" by All_Traffic.dest
| head 10
```
If zero results: the asset merge ran but the data model acceleration has not yet caught up. Wait for the acceleration to rebuild (check Settings > Data Models > Network_Traffic > Acceleration > Status).

**Proxy log field mapping:**
For Bluecoat (`sourcetype=bluecoat:proxystorage`), verify CIM field mapping:
```
index=proxy sourcetype=bluecoat:proxystorage earliest=-1h
| head 5
| table dest, src, bytes_out, app, action
```
If `bytes_out` is null, the TA may map it to `sc_bytes` or `cs_bytes`. Create a field alias in `props.conf`:
```ini
[bluecoat:proxystorage]
FIELDALIAS-bytes_out = sc_bytes AS bytes_out
```

For Zscaler (`sourcetype=zscaler:lss`), similar verification:
```
index=proxy sourcetype=zscaler:lss earliest=-1h
| head 5
| table dest, src, bytes_out, app, action
```
Zscaler's TA typically maps `upload_bytes` or `requestsize` to `bytes_out`. Check the TA's `props.conf` for existing aliases.

**Supplier risk lookup deployment:**
Place `supplier_risk_lookup.csv` in the app's lookups directory. Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[supplier_risk_lookup]
filename = supplier_risk_lookup.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```
Validate: `| inputlookup supplier_risk_lookup.csv | stats count dc(dest) as suppliers dc(risk_tier) as tiers`. Verify `suppliers` matches your third-party inventory. Verify `tiers` shows the expected distribution (e.g., 5 critical, 20 high, 50 medium, 100 low).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.16 NIS2 Supply Chain Risk Monitoring`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **`tstats` against the accelerated Network_Traffic data model**: this is the most efficient way to query large volumes of network traffic data. `tstats` operates on the pre-built acceleration summaries (TSIDX files), not raw events, making it orders of magnitude faster than a raw `index=proxy` search. For a typical enterprise generating 50 GB/day of proxy logs, a `tstats` query over 7 days completes in seconds; the equivalent raw search would take minutes.

• **`summariesonly` macro**: ``summariesonly`` resolves to `summariesonly=true` when acceleration is complete and `summariesonly=false` when it's incomplete. This is an ES convention that ensures the search still works during acceleration rebuild, albeit slower. If you're not using ES's macro, hardcode `summariesonly=true` for production and `summariesonly=false` for troubleshooting.

• **`sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out)` and `dc(All_Traffic.src)`**: two complementary metrics per supplier destination per day:
  - `bytes_out` (total outbound bytes) detects data exfiltration patterns. 1 GB/day (`1073741824` bytes) to a single supplier destination is the threshold — this is aggressive for some suppliers (e.g., cloud backup providers legitimately receive terabytes) but conservative for others (e.g., a consulting firm's SFTP server). Tune per supplier by adding a `baseline_bytes` column to the lookup.
  - `internal_sources` (distinct internal IPs communicating with the supplier) detects unusual access patterns. If 50 different internal IPs are talking to the same supplier API endpoint in one day, either the supplier is widely used (legitimate) or credentials have been compromised and lateral movement is reaching the supplier's infrastructure. The threshold of 50 is a starting point — adjust based on the supplier's expected usage pattern.

• **`span=1d`**: daily granularity. This smooths out bursty traffic patterns (e.g., a nightly backup job) while still detecting sustained high-volume transfers. For more sensitive monitoring, use `span=1h` but expect higher alert volume.

• **`lookup supplier_risk_lookup dest OUTPUT supplier_name, risk_tier, last_assessment_date`**: enriches each supplier destination with context from the risk lookup. The `dest` field from the `tstats` result is the join key. If the lookup uses FQDNs but `tstats` returns IPs (because the firewall logs IPs, not hostnames), the lookup will fail to match. Fix: maintain both `dest_ip` and `dest_fqdn` columns in the lookup and use: `| lookup supplier_risk_lookup dest_ip AS dest OUTPUT ...` then fallback `| lookup supplier_risk_lookup dest_fqdn AS dest OUTPUT ... OUTPUTNEW ...`.

• **Three independent alert conditions combined with OR**:
  1. `bytes_out > 1073741824` (1 GB/day outbound) — potential data exfiltration or unusually large data sharing.
  2. `assessment_overdue=1` (assessment older than 365 days) — governance gap. The 365-day threshold assumes annual assessment cadence; adjust for critical suppliers (180 days) or low-risk suppliers (730 days) by adding a `max_assessment_interval` column to the lookup and replacing `> 365` with `> max_assessment_interval`.
  3. `internal_sources > 50` (more than 50 distinct internal IPs) — unusual breadth of access. This may indicate credential sharing, compromised API keys, or a supplier integration that has grown beyond its original scope.

Schedule: cron `0 6 * * *` (daily at 06:00). Time range: use the `span=1d` built into the `tstats` query; recommend `earliest=-7d latest=now` for weekly context with daily granularity.

Alert actions: (1) For `risk_tier=critical` and any condition triggered: PagerDuty page to the CISO. (2) For `assessment_overdue=1` on any tier: email to procurement with the supplier name, risk tier, and days since last assessment. (3) For `bytes_out > 10737418240` (10 GB) to any supplier: create an ES notable event with `urgency=critical` for the SOC to investigate potential data exfiltration. (4) Write all results to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:supply_chain:traffic`.

Extended search — AWS cross-account monitoring:
If suppliers access your AWS environment, append:
```spl
| append [
    search index=cloud sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail eventName=AssumeRole earliest=-1d
    | search requestParameters.roleArn="*supplier*" OR requestParameters.roleArn="*vendor*" OR requestParameters.roleArn="*partner*"
    | stats count as role_assumptions dc(sourceIPAddress) as supplier_ips by requestParameters.roleArn
    | rename requestParameters.roleArn as dest
    | eval supplier_name="AWS Cross-Account: " . dest
]
```
This surfaces AWS IAM role assumptions by supplier accounts, providing visibility into cloud-based supply chain access.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Bytes parity check**: select a supplier from the results with high `bytes_out`. Identify the `dest` IP/FQDN. Run a raw search: `index=proxy dest="<IP>" earliest=-1d | stats sum(bytes_out) as raw_bytes`. Compare `raw_bytes` with the `tstats` result. They should match within 5% (minor differences from acceleration rebuild timing).

(b) **Assessment overdue parity**: select a supplier with `assessment_overdue=1`. Verify in the `supplier_risk_lookup.csv` that `last_assessment_date` is indeed more than 365 days ago. Calculate manually: `(today - last_assessment_date) / 86400`. If the lookup date is wrong, update it — this is a data maintenance issue, not a search issue.

(c) **Internal sources inflation test**: select a supplier with `internal_sources > 50`. Run: `index=proxy dest="<IP>" earliest=-1d | stats dc(src) as sources values(src) as src_list`. Verify `sources` matches `internal_sources` from `tstats`. If `sources` is much lower in the raw search, the CIM field mapping for `src` may be pulling the wrong field (e.g., the proxy's IP instead of the client's IP). Fix the field alias.

(d) **dest_category tagging verification**: run `| tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic where All_Traffic.dest_category="supplier" by All_Traffic.dest | sort - count`. Verify the top destinations are known suppliers. If unknown IPs appear, the asset merge has tagged non-supplier destinations — review the asset lookup for over-broad CIDR entries.

(e) **Negative test**: pick a known supplier with recent assessment (`last_assessment_date` within 60 days), low traffic (< 100 MB/day), and few internal sources (< 5). Verify they do NOT appear in the results. If they do, one of the three conditions is unexpectedly true — investigate.

(f) **CDN false positive test**: if a supplier uses a shared CDN (e.g., Akamai, CloudFront), the `dest` IP may be a CDN edge node that also serves non-supplier traffic. Run: `| tstats summariesonly=true dc(All_Traffic.dest_category) as categories from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic where All_Traffic.dest="<CDN-IP>" by All_Traffic.dest`. If `categories > 1`, the IP serves multiple purposes — refine the asset lookup to use TLS SNI (via Splunk Stream) or DNS-based classification instead of IP-based.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Supply Chain Risk Monitor`):
• **Row 1 — Headline tiles**: Single-value `Suppliers with overdue assessments` (red ≥ 1 for critical/high tier). Single-value `Total data transferred to suppliers (7d)` (formatted as TB/GB). Single-value `Suppliers exceeding upload threshold` (amber ≥ 1). Single-value `Average days since last assessment` (overall).
• **Row 2 — Timeline**: `| tstats summariesonly=true sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic where All_Traffic.dest_category="supplier" by _time All_Traffic.dest span=1d | timechart sum(bytes_out) by dest limit=10`. Shows daily traffic volume to top 10 suppliers over time — spikes indicate unusual data transfers.
• **Row 3 — Assessment calendar**: table of all suppliers with `supplier_name`, `risk_tier`, `last_assessment_date`, `days_since_assessment`, `assessment_overdue`. Colour-code: green (within 80% of cycle), amber (80-100% of cycle), red (overdue). This is the procurement team's primary working view.
• **Row 4 — Top uploaders**: bar chart of `bytes_out` by `supplier_name` for the last 7 days. Annotate with `risk_tier` colour coding. A high-risk supplier receiving large data volumes is the worst combination.
• **Row 5 — Internal source analysis**: for each supplier, show `internal_sources` as a column. High values indicate widely used supplier integrations — these are high-impact suppliers if compromised.

Runbook (owner: Procurement / Third-Party Risk Management):
1. Daily: review alert. For each triggered row:
   - `assessment_overdue=1`: contact procurement to schedule the overdue assessment. If the supplier is `risk_tier=critical`, escalate to the CISO within 24 hours — operating without a current assessment for a critical supplier violates Art.21(2)(d).
   - `bytes_out > 1 GB`: verify the data transfer is expected. Check the business justification: is there a data sharing agreement (DSA) in place? Does the DSA cover this volume? If not, escalate to the data protection officer (DPO).
   - `internal_sources > 50`: verify the access pattern. Is the supplier's API endpoint used by a legitimate internal application that runs on many servers? Or is this credential leakage?
2. Weekly: review the assessment calendar. Send reminders to procurement for assessments due within 30 days.
3. Monthly: generate the supply chain risk report. Include: (a) assessment coverage percentage (assessed/total), (b) overdue assessment list, (c) data flow summary by risk tier, (d) any anomalies detected. Sign with RFC3161 and archive.
4. After any supply chain security incident (e.g., a vendor breach): immediately run the search with `earliest=-90d` to assess historical exposure. The `bytes_out` trend for the compromised supplier shows what data may have been accessible.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **`tstats` returns zero results — no supplier traffic.** The Network_Traffic data model acceleration is not built or `dest_category=supplier` is not populated. Check acceleration status: Settings > Data Models > Network_Traffic > Acceleration > Status (should show "Complete" or "Building"). If "Not accelerated", enable it. If "Building", wait. If acceleration is complete but `dest_category` is null, the ES asset merge has not tagged supplier destinations — verify the asset lookup is loaded and the merge has run: `| rest /servicesNS/-/SplunkEnterpriseSecuritySuite/admin/macros/asset_sources_lookup_name | table title`.

• **All `days_since_assessment` are null — `strptime` returns null.** The `last_assessment_date` format in the CSV doesn't match `%Y-%m-%d`. Common issues: (a) European date format `15/06/2025` — fix: `strptime(last_assessment_date, "%d/%m/%Y")`. (b) Date with time `2025-06-15T10:00:00Z` — fix: `strptime(last_assessment_date, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")`. (c) Excel-exported dates with locale-specific formatting. Test: `| makeresults | eval d="15/06/2025" | eval e=strptime(d, "%d/%m/%Y") | table e`.

• **`bytes_out` always zero despite proxy traffic.** The proxy TA is not mapping the upload bytes field to the CIM `bytes_out`. Check: `index=proxy sourcetype=bluecoat:proxystorage earliest=-1h | head 5 | table bytes_out sc_bytes cs_bytes sent_bytes`. Identify which raw field contains the upload byte count and create a FIELDALIAS in `props.conf`.

• **CDN shared IP inflates multiple supplier byte counts.** A single Akamai edge IP (`23.x.x.x`) serves traffic for multiple suppliers. The `tstats` aggregates all traffic to that IP as one "supplier." Fix: use DNS-based classification instead of IP-based. Configure Splunk Stream to capture TLS SNI headers, which provide the destination FQDN even for HTTPS traffic. Update the supplier asset lookup to use FQDNs instead of IPs.

• **Supplier uses dynamic IP ranges (cloud-hosted).** Supplier APIs hosted on AWS/Azure/GCP have rotating IP addresses. The asset lookup's static IP entries become stale. Fix: (a) Use FQDN-based matching via DNS proxy logs (the `dest` field will contain the FQDN). (b) For IP-based matching, maintain the supplier's published IP ranges (e.g., from `ip-ranges.amazonaws.com`) and update weekly. (c) Use Splunk ES's "IP Intelligence" framework to auto-update from threat intelligence feeds that track cloud provider IP ranges.

• **Assessment overdue for a supplier that was assessed last week.** The `supplier_risk_lookup.csv` has not been updated after the assessment. This is a process issue, not a technical issue. Fix: automate CSV refresh from the GRC tool. If using ServiceNow, schedule a scripted input that queries `/api/now/table/vendor_risk_assessment?sysparm_query=state=completed` and updates the CSV with the latest `completed_date`. If using a spreadsheet-based process (common in smaller organisations), assign a named owner and set a calendar reminder.

• **Internal sources count inflated by NAT.** If internal clients are NATed to a small number of IPs before reaching the proxy, `dc(All_Traffic.src)` counts the NAT IPs, not the actual client IPs. The `internal_sources` metric becomes meaningless (e.g., always 2-3 for NAT pool IPs). Fix: ensure proxy logs contain the `X-Forwarded-For` header and map it to `src` in `props.conf`. For firewall-only environments without proxy, this may not be fixable — document the limitation and rely on `bytes_out` as the primary metric.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on how much information flows out to outside partners and whether anyone forgot to renew the formal safety review for those partners on time.
